preamble: The phonetic transformation from the voiced postalveolar fricative [ʒ] to the voiceless alveolar fricative [s] is a documented sound change that involves both devoicing and fronting. This shift can be observed in various linguistic contexts, including historical language contact, dialectal evolution, and as a common pattern of L1 interference in second language acquisition.

examples.note: An early historical shift of /ʒ/ to /s/ occurred in French loanwords borrowed into Dutch and Afrikaans.
This phonological change is attested in the Welsh English dialect.
Lamaholot speakers learning English frequently replace the voiced postalveolar fricative [ʒ] with the voiceless alveolar fricative [s], for instance, in words like 'television'.
Sundanese speakers consistently transform the English sound /ʒ/ to /s/ due to L1 interference.
Javanese EFL learners exhibit phonological fossilization, commonly replacing /ʒ/ with /s/, as seen in the pronunciation of 'pleasure'.
Some Arabic dialects show a shift of /ʒ/ to /s/ or /z/.

sources: From Cape Dutch to Afrikaans A Comparison of Phonemic Inventories - Utrecht University Student Theses Repository Home.
A History of the Welsh English Dialect in Fiction - Cronfa - Swansea University.
Segmental Pronunciation Errors Made by the Lamaholot Speakers of Junior High School Students - DigitalOcean.
the analysis of sundanese language to students' english consonant pronunciation conducted in sma mutiara palabuhan ratu - ResearchGate.
Phonological Fossilisation of EFL Learners: The Interference of Phonological and Orthographic System of L1 Javanese - ResearchGate.
